event: Dormition and Assumption of the Virgin
symbols: mandorla surrounding Mary — divine glory and the heavenly realm enveloping the Theotokos
gold background — the uncreated light of God and eternity beyond earthly time
empty bier below, glorified Mary above — triumph over death through divine grace
triangular gable — the Trinity sheltering the mystery of the Assumption
angels flanking the ascent — heavenly host escorting the Queen of Heaven
apostles gathered around the deathbed — the universal Church witnessing the Dormition
description: A two-register panel set within a pointed gable frame with gold ornamental borders. The lower register shows the apostles and saints gathered around the deathbed of the Virgin Mary, whose body lies on a bier draped in white. Christ stands behind the bier holding a small swaddled figure representing Mary's soul. The upper register depicts Mary standing upright within a blue mandorla, clothed in white, surrounded by angels and saints in brilliant robes of red, blue, green, and gold. At the apex, a red seraph or divine figure presides beneath the golden triangular gable.
theological_description: This dual-register composition presents the two inseparable mysteries of Marian theology: the Dormition (Koimesis) and the Assumption (Transitus). The lower scene follows the ancient tradition recorded in pseudo-Dionysius and John of Damascus: the apostles are miraculously transported to Jerusalem for Mary's passing, while Christ receives her soul — reversing the Incarnation, where Mary received Christ into her body. The upper register shows Mary's bodily glorification, taken up into heaven as the first fruits of the general resurrection (1 Cor 15:20-23 applied typologically). The mandorla of blue — heaven's color — contrasts with the gold of divine eternity surrounding it. Patristically, Mary is the new Ark of the Covenant (Rev 11:19; Ps 132:8), and just as the Ark was brought into the Holy of Holies, so Mary is assumed into heavenly glory. The triangular architectural frame signals Trinitarian theology. This image functions as a reliquary tabernacle — a liturgical object housing sacred relics — making the painting itself an act of veneration, linking the visual theology of the Assumption to the sacramental presence of the saints.

scripture_refs.reason: The angel's greeting 'full of grace' undergirds Mary's unique role culminating in her Assumption
The woman clothed with the sun — patristic type of Mary glorified in heaven
'Arise, O Lord, to your resting place, you and the ark of your might' — Mary as the Ark assumed into the heavenly sanctuary
Christ the firstfruits of resurrection; Mary's Assumption as anticipation of the general resurrection
The Ark of the Covenant seen in the heavenly temple — patristic link to Mary's entry into heaven
The Magnificat — Mary's soul magnifies the Lord, fulfilled in her heavenly glorification
